judge_rationale: Exceptional solution: functionally correct for all test cases, demonstrates deep understanding of lazy initialization for mocking compatibility, proper thread-safe locking, continuous token refill calculation, and capacity enforcement. Code is publication-quality with clear insights explaining design choices.

Complete, correct implementation that passes all test cases. Thread-safe with proper lock usage, lazy initialization elegantly handles mocking constraints, and continuous refill logic is accurate. Explanation and trace-through validate the approach thoroughly.
Correct, complete implementation with proper thread-safety and continuous refill; minor code duplication between allow() and available_tokens() methods is the only gap.
Correct and complete async retry implementation with clear explanation; unreachable code at end is minor style issue.

Complete, correct implementation that precisely follows the specification; exponential backoff formula matches requirements exactly; all test cases pass; proper async/await and error handling; includes helpful educational commentary.

Exceptional implementation: correct exponential backoff formula, proper jitter application, correct retry logic with status-code filtering, and all test cases verified to pass. Code is clean, well-commented, properly async/awaits, and demonstrates full understanding of the requirements.

Correct and complete consistent hash ring implementation with O(log n) binary search for get_node. All test cases should pass. Minor deduction: add_node/remove_node use O(n) list operations (bisect.insort and list.remove) instead of more efficient data structures like a balanced tree.

Flawless implementation with correct bisect_left logic for O(log n) clockwise node lookup, proper idempotency via set tracking, correct wrapping behavior, and all test cases should pass including non-trivial distribution uniformity.

Implementation is correct, complete, and efficient. Uses bisect for O(log n) lookups, properly handles idempotency, ring wrapping, and should pass all tests including distribution uniformity. Code is clear with good design decisions explained in the insight.

Excellent comprehensive analysis with perfect time/space complexity reasoning, but two edge case outputs are incorrect: negative numbers case produces [(0, 2)] not [(-5, 0), (0, 1)], and multiple solutions case produces [(2, 3), (0, 4)] not [(0, 2), (3, 4)].

Exceptional analysis covering all required dimensions with accurate complexity claims, detailed identification of the critical duplicate-handling bug with execution traces, comprehensive edge-case coverage, algorithm comparisons, and proposed fixes. Minor redundancy in explanations but overall professional presentation with strong justification throughout.

Exceptional submission—all complexity claims are mathematically correct, all five required analysis points thoroughly addressed, critical bug elegantly identified with concrete traces and fixes, brute-force comparison practical and accurate, structure professional with tables and code examples.

Exceptional implementation. Correct Pratt parser with all binding powers, associativity rules, and error cases handled properly. Handles floats (.5, 1., etc.), unary minus precedence (-2**2 = -4), right-associative **, and all edge cases. Code is well-organized, clearly explained, and all test cases pass.

Correct and complete Pratt parser with sound logic: tokenization handles all numeric formats, nud/led properly implement operator precedence and associativity, unary minus correctly calls expression(2) to allow ** (bp=3) to bind tighter, all error cases covered, and all test cases pass. Minor deduction only for lack of inline code comments.

The Pratt parser approach is sound and the code handles most cases correctly (tokenization, precedence, associativity), but contains a critical bug: unary minus with exponentiation produces incorrect results (-2**2 returns 4 instead of -4), because calling parse_expression(4) from unary minus prevents ** from being parsed as part of the operand. The explanation also contradicts the test requirement.
Excellent submission with correct O(1) LFU implementation, all test cases passing, proper use of OrderedDict for LRU tiebreaking, sound min_freq tracking, and clear explanation of design choices.
Correct O(1) LFU cache with proper frequency-to-keys mapping, LRU tie-breaking, and min_freq tracking. All test cases pass. Well-structured code with clear explanation and helpful insight block.

Complete, correct O(1) LFU implementation with proper LRU tie-breaking using OrderedDict and frequency tracking, handles all edge cases flawlessly, excellent data structure choices, and includes clear educational insights explaining design decisions.

Passes all visible tests with correct thread-safe logic, but the tokens parameter type hint is int instead of float (specification mismatch), and integer truncation in token arithmetic may lose precision with fractional rates.

Core async logic and exponential backoff are sound and would pass the provided tests, but jitter implementation uses (0.9, 1.1) instead of spec's (0.5, 1.5), default max_delay is 60 not 30, and retryable_status parameter type mismatches spec (frozenset vs tuple).
Correct, complete, well-documented consistent hash ring implementation using MD5 and bisect for O(log n) lookups; properly handles idempotency, edge cases, and virtual node distribution.

Correct O(1) LFU implementation using OrderedDict for LRU tie-breaking within frequency buckets; handles all test cases including frequency promotion, min_freq updates, and edge cases; well-documented with clear data structures and helper methods.

Complete and correct analysis of all five required points with thorough coverage of edge cases; identifies a real correctness issue (duplicate handling) and provides fix recommendations; exceptionally well-structured with clear traces, comparison tables, and balanced discussion of theory vs. practice.

Complete, correct implementation of all test cases using recursive descent (acceptable alternative to Pratt); proper operator precedence, associativity, and comprehensive error handling; clean code structure and adequate documentation.

Implementation is correct, complete, and well-documented. Proper use of MD5 hashing, binary search via bisect for O(log n) lookups, correct virtual node handling with idempotency, and all edge cases properly addressed. Minor unused import (Optional) is negligible.

Functionally correct implementation that should pass all tests. Properly handles exponential backoff, jitter, retryable status codes, and error handling. Minor issues: unused last_response variable, confusing unnecessary inspect.iscoroutine() defensive check, and minor deviation from spec (uses 2^(attempt-1) rather than 2^attempt for backoff formula, though this is arguably better practice and tests won't catch it since base_delay=0).
Complete and correct implementation passing all tests with proper thread safety; minor code duplication in refill logic prevents perfect score.

Correct logic and clean implementation that handles all LFU/LRU eviction cases properly, but fails to meet the explicit O(1) time requirement because it uses min(freq_to_keys.keys()) instead of maintaining the min_freq tracker.

Exceptional submission: complete recursive descent parser correctly implementing all operator precedence, associativity rules, and error handling; properly handles the subtle -2**2=-4 case with right-associative exponentiation; all 21 tests pass; code is well-structured, clearly commented, and robust.

Comprehensive, correct analysis addressing all five required dimensions with thorough coverage, concrete examples, practical guidance, and identified issues with mitigations. Excellent organization, formatting, and clarity. Minor notational inconsistency in one edge-case example (expected pairs notation doesn't match array values), but the conceptual point about index overwriting is valid. This is professional-quality work that exceeds base requirements.

Exceptional solution that passes all 8 tests with correct implementation of consistent hashing, excellent documentation, and clean code; minor inefficiency in remove_node (O(m*n) via list.remove rather than optimized O(m log n)) prevents perfect score despite meeting all stated requirements.
Passes all tests with correct exponential backoff, jitter, and retry logic; strong implementation undermined by convoluted mock-handling code and overly broad exception catching.

Correctly implements most of the token bucket specification with excellent code clarity and structure; passes 3 of 4 tests but fails thread_safety test due to real-time refill behavior allowing more tokens than capacity during concurrent execution.

Flawless O(1) LFU implementation. All three data structures (key_to_val, key_to_freq, freq_to_keys with OrderedDict) used correctly. min_freq tracking is sound: only increments when a frequency bucket becomes empty. LRU tiebreaking via OrderedDict insertion order works correctly. Passes all 8 tests including edge cases (capacity=1, multi-eviction sequences, LRU tiebreak with get()). Code is well-documented with clear method docstrings and variable names. Proper use of stdlib only.

Correctly implements a complete Pratt parser with proper operator precedence, associativity, and comprehensive error handling. All test cases pass. Minor issues: unreachable return statement in evaluate() and slightly confusing precedence explanation in docstring.

Exceptional analysis with accurate complexity claims, correct bug identification in seen[num] overwriting, comprehensive edge case coverage (7 cases with traces), detailed algorithmic comparison with benchmarks, and professional formatting. Exceeds all five required analysis points with depth and rigor. Minor deduction for some sections being slightly verbose without adding material value.
